title: New leader  in political spending emerges
summary: The politically connected Anchorage real estate development firm Penco was Alaska's biggest contributor to congressional races over the past year, moving into a top spot previously held by the defunct Veco Corp. There were $51,100 in donations associated with Penco in 2007, the latest Federal Election Commission reports available. That's close to the $51,650 that Veco managers gave for congressional races in 2006 before top executives pleaded guilty to bribing state legislators and sold the company. Veco was top Alaska donor for the past decade to federal campaigns, mostly congressional races, the Center for Responsive Politics found. Penco is a family business owned by Bob Penney, a sportfishing activist who is a longtime friend and past business partner of Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens. Penney…

title: Police: Shooting linked to burglary
summary: A shootout between two cars driving south on Minnesota Drive early Friday morning left no one injured but gave police a break on a $100,000 burglary they were working to solve, according to Anchorage police. Police were preparing to get a search warrant Friday for a residence that detectives suspected was connected to the burglary that netted guns, cash and jewelry, police Lt. Paul Honeman said. But the shooting, which involved at least one of the people who lived in the home on Oregon Drive in Spenard, erupted about 4 a.m., before officers could conduct the search, he said. The burst of gunfire appears to have been between two groups that knew each other and were having a dispute, possibly over a stolen vehicle, Honeman said. There were no immediate reports of damage or injuries. Police q…

title: 55-year-old man's goal is to bike to Nome in Iditarod Invitational
summary: R ocky Reifenstuhl is finally going to Nome. After 20 years of racing on the first 350 miles of the Iditarod Trail, Fairbanks' favorite pedal pusher is attempting to ride the entire 1,100-mile trail to Nome in this year's Iditarod Invitational, the world's longest human-powered race. By Wednesday morning, he was among a group of four leading cyclists who had left Ruby, more than halfway to Nome. About 500 miles of pedaling -- and pushing -- remained. "I figure I'm not getting any younger," the 55-year-old Reifenstuhl said. "If I'm going to do it, I need to do it." Having competed in shorter 100-, 200- and 350-mile races every winter for the last 20 years, Reifenstuhl has pedaled more miles across the frozen Alaska wilderness than any biker in the state. In race mileage alone, Reifenstuhl h…

title: Change on the Kenai River can't be dammed up
summary: Change is a glacial river. Depending on the season, it might flow more or less, but it always flows. Stopping it is impossible, but with careful effort it can be steered. Some rip rap here can bend it. Some channelization there can straighten it. A dam or two or more can regulate the speed of its current. The Kenai is a glacial river fortunately undammed -- and, even more fortunately, protected from any future dams. More than 20 years ago, this paper did a lengthy series on the slow but steady environmental degradation of the river and detailed the social, economic and politic problems then standing in the way of its preservation as one of the finest salmon streams in the state. That series, in part, helped lead to creation of the Kenai River Special Management Area as a division of Alaska…

title: Man accused of punching trooper
summary: WASILLA -- Alaska State Trooper Todd Womack was punched in the face just after midnight Wednesday when he stopped to investigate a Ford Bronco in a ditch, troopers reported Wednesday. After a brief struggle, Womack was able to restrain the driver, Leland Kimble, 21, of Willow. Kimble was still inside the Bronco when Womack arrived, according to troopers. Kimble was arrested for driving under the influence, fourth-degree assault and resisting arrest. The incident took place at 2030 Lucille St. in Wasilla. Kimble was jailed at the Mat-Su Pre-Trial Facility in lieu of $750 bail and a court-approved third party custodian.

title: All abuzz about Palin
summary: WASHINGTON -- Gov. Sarah Palin will be the first to admit that it might be a stretch for a hockey mom from Alaska to be considered for the No. 2 spot on Sen. John McCain's presidential ticket. But there's an undeniable national buzz surrounding the first-term governor, seen by many Republicans both within Alaska and outside the state as a fresh, new face to represent the party's future. The vice presidency may be far-fetched, but the hype has only helped Palin's future political prospects -- and Alaska's reputation. "This happens almost every cycle, that the great mentioner starts spinning all sorts of names, some of which are more realistic than others," said Joel Goldstein, a law professor at Saint Louis University who has written a book on the vice presidency. "Even for somebody who doe…

title: Baby news strikes a chord
summary: When Anchorage investment broker Marietta Hall got to the pool early Thursday morning, her female friends were all talking about Sarah Palin's baby news. She's seven months along? Unbelievable. They guessed why she waited to tell the public: There's only two months left for people to make "it's just her hormones" jokes to undercut her decisions. "Thank goodness she waited this long," said Hall, who had her first son six months ago. "That would have been annoying." Palin's announcement has sparked conversations among women across the state about sexism, economics, ambition and the age-old conflict between family and work. It made Hall reflect on own life. She knows Palin's situation isn't as easy as it sounds. Hall waited to establish her career before having her son, Aidan, at 38. She's se…
